title: Why Leadership Is a Behavior (Not a Title) + Credit Confidence for Women
guestBio: Elizabeth Taylor, known as @thecorporatebestie, is a leadership trainer, sales coach, and organizational development expert who specializes in helping high-performing women transition into management roles without burning out. She combines her expertise in leadership development with her background in the credit industry to help women build both professional confidence and financial literacy.
excerpt: Leadership isn't about your title—it's about how you show up. Elizabeth Taylor breaks down why women managers are the most burned-out group and shares the financial literacy skills every leader needs to build generational wealth.
metaDescription: Learn why leadership is a behavior, not a title. Elizabeth Taylor shares how women can lead without burnout plus credit confidence tips for financial freedom.
keyTakeaways: Leadership is fundamentally a behavior and mindset, not a position or title you hold.
Women managers between 35-45 experience the highest rates of burnout and need specific strategies to combat this epidemic.
Developing a clear leadership brand helps you show up consistently and confidently in your role.
Credit confidence is a crucial but underrated leadership skill that directly impacts your freedom and opportunities.
Being risk-aware rather than risk-averse allows women to make informed financial decisions with confidence.
The current generational wealth transfer presents unprecedented opportunities for women who are financially prepared.
Balancing ambition with rest and joy is essential for sustainable leadership success.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership isn't a title — it's a behavior.

timestamps.label: Why women managers are burning out

content:
What if everything you've been told about leadership is wrong? What if it's not about climbing the corporate ladder, collecting impressive titles, or managing the biggest team? Elizabeth Taylor, better known as @thecorporatebestie, is here to flip the script entirely.

In this eye-opening conversation with Aimee, Elizabeth reveals a truth that will reshape how you think about your career: leadership isn't a title you earn—it's a behavior you choose. And that choice? It can transform not just your professional life, but your financial future too.

The Burnout Epidemic Nobody's Talking About

Let's start with a sobering reality check. Women managers between the ages of 35 and 45 are experiencing burnout at unprecedented rates. You might be nodding your head right now, feeling seen in a way that's both validating and exhausting. Elizabeth doesn't just acknowledge this crisis—she provides a roadmap out of it.

The problem isn't that women are weak or incapable of handling leadership pressure. The issue is systemic. We're often promoted into management roles without proper training, expected to figure it out as we go, all while managing households, caring for aging parents, and trying to maintain some semblance of personal identity. Sound familiar?

Elizabeth's approach is refreshingly different. She believes that when you understand leadership as a behavior rather than a position, you can start showing up differently immediately. You don't need to wait for someone to hand you a fancy title or corner office. You can begin leading today, in whatever role you're in, with whatever resources you have.

Your Leadership Brand Is Your Superpower

Here's where Elizabeth's wisdom gets really practical. She talks about developing what she calls a "leadership brand"—the consistent way you show up, make decisions, and interact with others. This isn't about personal branding in the Instagram-influencer sense. This is about intentional consistency that builds trust and respect over time.

Think about the leaders you most admire. Chances are, you can predict how they'll handle certain situations because they've established clear patterns of behavior. They're not reinventing themselves every day or trying to be someone different in every meeting. They know who they are as leaders, and they show up authentically within that framework.

Your leadership brand becomes your anchor when everything else feels chaotic. When you're clear on your values, decision-making style, and communication approach, you can navigate challenges with confidence instead of second-guessing every choice.

The Money Conversation We're Not Having

But Elizabeth doesn't stop at traditional leadership advice. She brings something unique to the table: a deep understanding of how financial literacy intersects with leadership effectiveness. This is where the conversation gets really interesting—and really important.

She argues that credit confidence is one of the most underrated leadership skills, and she's absolutely right. When you understand credit, you understand risk assessment, financial planning, and strategic thinking. These are the exact skills that make you a better leader in any context.

More importantly, financial literacy gives you freedom. Freedom to take calculated risks in your career. Freedom to negotiate from a position of strength. Freedom to build the kind of generational wealth that changes everything for your family.

Risk-Aware, Not Risk-Averse

Elizabeth makes a crucial distinction that every woman needs to hear: there's a difference between being risk-averse and being risk-aware. Society has conditioned many women to avoid risk altogether, but that's not serving us. Smart leaders aren't afraid of risk—they're informed about it.

This mindset shift is profound. When you're risk-aware, you ask better questions. You gather more information. You make decisions based on data rather than fear. You start to see opportunities where others see obstacles.

This applies whether you're considering a career move, starting a business, making an investment, or even having difficult conversations at work. Risk-awareness gives you permission to be bold while still being smart.

The Wealth Transfer Moment

Here's something that might surprise you: we're in the middle of the largest generational wealth transfer in history. Elizabeth points out that women who are financially prepared for this moment have unprecedented opportunities ahead of them.

But preparation is key. You can't benefit from opportunities you don't understand or aren't positioned to take advantage of. This is why Elizabeth connects leadership development with financial education—they're not separate skills, they're complementary ones.

Permission to Rest and Lead

Perhaps the most radical thing Elizabeth advocates for is this: you can be ambitious and prioritize rest and joy. These aren't mutually exclusive. In fact, sustainable leadership requires both.

Too many women are burning out because they think leadership means constant sacrifice and perpetual hustle. Elizabeth offers a different model—one where taking care of yourself isn't selfish, it's strategic. When you're operating from a place of rest and joy, you make better decisions, communicate more effectively, and inspire others naturally.

This isn't about work-life balance in the traditional sense. It's about integration. It's about showing up as a whole person who happens to be a leader, rather than trying to compartmentalize yourself into unsustainable fragments.

The truth Elizabeth shares throughout this conversation is both simple and revolutionary: you already have what it takes to lead. You don't need permission from anyone else, another degree, or a different personality. You need clarity on your values, confidence in your abilities, and the courage to show up consistently as the leader you already are.

Leadership is a behavior. Financial literacy is power. And you? You're more capable than you know.

quiz.title: What's Your Leadership Style DNA?
quiz.description: Leadership isn't about your title—it's about how you show up every single day. Discover your natural leadership approach and how to leverage it for success.

questions.question: When you're facing a major decision at work, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Consider how this will impact each person on your team individually
Look for the strategic opportunity that others might be missing
Gather data and think through potential long-term consequences
Trust your gut while staying flexible about the outcome

questions.question: Your relationship with financial risk could best be described as:

options.text: I'll take risks for others' benefit, but rarely for my own financial gain
I see financial opportunities where others see obstacles
I prefer building wealth slowly through informed, steady choices
I adjust my financial strategy based on what life throws at me

questions.question: When someone asks you to describe your leadership style, you'd probably say:

options.text: I lead by caring deeply and making sure everyone feels supported
I lead by helping people see what's possible and inspiring action
I lead by being consistent, reliable, and following through on commitments
I lead by meeting people where they are and adjusting to what they need

questions.question: You're most likely to feel burned out when:

options.text: You've been giving so much to everyone else that you've forgotten about yourself
You're stuck in routines that don't challenge you or move the needle forward
You're being pressured to make quick decisions without proper analysis
You're forced into rigid structures that don't allow for flexibility

questions.question: Your ideal work environment would allow you to:

options.text: Build deep relationships and create a culture where everyone thrives
Think big, take calculated risks, and drive meaningful change
Work systematically toward clear goals with adequate resources
Pivot when needed and apply your skills to diverse challenges

questions.question: When it comes to balancing ambition with rest and joy, you:

options.text: Struggle to rest because you're always thinking about how to help others
Find joy in pursuing ambitious goals, but sometimes forget to celebrate wins
Believe rest and planning go hand-in-hand for sustainable success
Adapt your definition of balance based on what each season of life requires

results.title: The Fierce Protector
results.description: You lead with your heart and fierce loyalty to your team. Your superpower is creating psychological safety where others can thrive, but you often sacrifice your own needs in the process. You're the manager everyone wants to work for, yet you're probably experiencing that burnout epidemic Elizabeth talks about. This episode will show you how to channel your protective instincts into sustainable leadership that doesn't drain your energy.

results.title: The Strategic Visionary
results.description: You see possibilities everywhere and aren't afraid to take calculated risks to get there. Your natural leadership style is about painting the big picture and inspiring others to join your mission. You understand that leadership is a behavior, not a title, and you've probably been leading long before anyone gave you permission. This episode will help you develop the credit confidence and financial literacy to turn your bold visions into reality.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You believe in consistent progress over flashy moves, and your leadership style reflects that wisdom. You're naturally risk-aware rather than risk-averse, making thoughtful decisions that build long-term success. Your team trusts you because you show up the same way every day, creating that leadership brand Elizabeth emphasizes. This episode will validate your approach while showing you how to amplify your quiet confidence in bigger ways.

results.title: The Adaptive Navigator
results.description: You're the leader who thrives in uncertainty and helps others find their footing when everything feels chaotic. Your strength lies in reading situations quickly and adjusting your approach to meet the moment. You intuitively understand that leadership is about behavior and mindset, not hierarchy. This episode will give you frameworks to harness your adaptability while building the financial confidence to navigate any storm.

transcript:
**Aimee:** All right, Elizabeth, it's so nice to have you today. So looking forward to having you on the show and gaining some of your amazing knowledge and passing that on to our listeners today. Thank you so much for being here.

**Elizabeth:** Well, thank you for having me. I jump at these opportunities. I just really believe in women supporting women. I really believe that we all need a little bit of push in the direction of confidence and advocacy and self-improvement. And so I'm really thrilled to be here.

**Aimee:** So glad you're here. So your nickname is the corporate bestie. And so I'd love for you to expand on that. What does that mean to you and why do you think so many women managers need one?

**Elizabeth:** Yeah. So, you know, I am coming out of an 11-year tenure in corporate and I've been working for commercial companies since I was 16 years old. And so corporate policy, attention to detail, execution, standards, these are things that came very natural to me, but also in the roles and functions that I was in within the business, I very quickly became everyone's go-to. Liz is your bestie. She's going to be the person that knows everyone or she can put you in the direction of someone who does. She knows all of the information.

And so it's really important to just level up ourselves when we think about our leadership brand, how we lead teams, how we influence within a business. And that's really where the moniker of the corporate bestie comes from. You know, first-time people managers are often promoted because they are high-performing individual contributors who are just crushing it, right? And what ends up happening is the business expects them to scale their behaviors. But in reality it's people's skills, right? It's not just training to the tactical how someone needs to do their job. There's a lot of soft skill development or power skill development a mentor of mine calls it.

And you know, first-time managers really need more of that. And so yeah, that's where I come in. I help be that beacon of light, that guidance, and help really set others up for success in that way.

**Aimee:** I love that. I think you're so right. I mean, I know myself as the boss lady, you see somebody who's a high performer and you say, "You should have a team under you." We do it all the time, right? But we're not really considering the fact that the soft skills that go into it. You know, just because you're a great sales person doesn't mean that you're going to be the most amazing leader and teacher, you know, so having those different kinds of skills. It's a very different skill set. You're right.

**Elizabeth:** Well, and funny enough, that's exactly what it is. You know, just because you're an awesome seller, you're hitting your quota, you're doing all of the revenue things, you're hitting all of your goals, you still need to be able to speak to people and motivate them and communicate differently with them. And what Aimee needs is very different from what Liz needs. And so, how do you adjust that? You know, there's secret sauce to everybody. And so, a really great leader is able to tap into that very well.

**Aimee:** I love that. Yeah, I love that. What's the common trap that people fall into? So, we're hiring these people with these skills and we are falling into this trap of putting these people in this leadership position and then we're expecting them to be able to manage and it's not working. I've seen it.

**Elizabeth:** Yeah. Well, and that is the trap, right? That is the trap. There's this expectation or it's an expectation that's not even communicated. Even if you're hiring someone outside of your company, say you're not even promoting internally, they're coming in with this kind of understood set of skills because they've led people before somewhere else. But it doesn't work that way. And just because someone's gone through a manager training or a development program doesn't mean that they've really honed in on those skills either.

Behavior change doesn't happen because you've attended an eight-hour workshop or because you did a deep dive for a week at some offsite at some beautiful retreat with a very lovely six-figure organization that comes in, right? Behavior change happens when you're working on something very intentionally and methodically over time. And that's where we really see success and that's how you avoid the trap.

**Aimee:** I love it. Your eight-week training is for first-time managers and focuses on helping them define their leadership brand, which I love. So, can you explain what that is and why it matters?

**Elizabeth:** Leadership brand is really about how are you perceived? How do you influence, inspire, and motivate teams? What do people say about you when you are not in a room? You know, we've all had managers that if I say the word authoritative, someone comes to mind for you. If I say servant leader, someone comes to mind for you. If I say transactional leader, someone's probably popping in your head. Those words are, you know, they mean something to us and they align to behaviors, right? And so that's where the leadership brand is really, really important.

And what I love about leadership brand is your brand should really tie to your core values as to who you are as a human. We should not be spending time on things that don't serve us as people. And I understand that that's very much easier said than done when we're trying to pay bills and take care of the kids and do the laundry and get the cleaning done and the cooking and oh we got spouses and oh we have girlfriends and then oh we also have ourselves to take care of right so it's very easy to say oh well if it doesn't serve me I can't do that but we really have to make sure that the way we're leading others the way we're inspiring others is in alignment with what feels good to us because then we can properly be put in roles that allow us to do that intuitively and very well if that makes sense.

**Aimee:** That totally makes sense. Yeah. So I get that. Absolutely. And okay, we had this interesting discussion and we talked about how managers are the most burnt out group and that most are women between the ages of 35 and 45. So why do you think that women in that stage of life are so vulnerable to burnout?

**Elizabeth:** Well, you know, there's a lot of data out there that suggests what that is, but I'll share my two cents and what I've observed and what I talk to with my clients. You know, we are brought up with this expectation that we have to be a certain kind of person that's really other than ourselves. Whether it's the people pleaser, the perfectionist, the type A, the control, whatever the adjective you want to give it, there is some kind of expectation.

And so we are learning at 35 to 45 how to really step in and own and reclaim our lives. And I think what's really cool is that when you know between 35 and 45 we're no longer these early career professionals. You know maybe we've climbed a little bit of the corporate ladder and we're seeing what that experience is like and we're deciding again going back to does this serve me right?

And then we have kids that are growing up. We also have parents that are aging. We are a very unique demographic of people that perhaps didn't have the same challenges that our prior generations did. And so between just work expectations, life expectations, our own expectations, there is too much on our plates. And so that's why we're seeing so many women specifically get burnt out.

And then when you take a look at the business side of things, right, we're not really equipping this group of women with the resources that they need to be successful. We're measuring them against what other successful people have done that have come before them when it's not even the same kind of playing field. The standards are very different. The goalpost continues to move as far as what is achievable, what we need in women, right?

Women are leaving corporate at a record rate right now. And it's because we're able to go, "Oh my gosh, I don't need this. Then there is something that I can better do with my time." And so, you know, there's a lot that goes into the burnout, but I think the really cool conversation is how do we become who do we become, right? Once we decide that's not something that I want, but I think there's something over here that does, right? And again, that's why leadership brand is so important as well.

**Aimee:** I love that. Is that what happened to you? Is that how you ended up as the corporate bestie? Did you burn out?

**Elizabeth:** I think that I certainly had moments of burnout and I had a lot going on in my personal life. I really honestly I always wanted to be in the C-suite Aimee. I always wanted to be a chief learning officer. So my background is in learning and development. I have a strong focus in sales enablement and I thought it would be so cool to set the learning and development strategy for an organization and the organization that I was working with at the time they just didn't value learning in the same way and that's totally okay right like a business doesn't have to prioritize the same things that I need to prioritize and I just I kind of woke up one day and you know the leaders that I admired weren't really there anymore the culture had changed it just didn't feel good to do the things that I once love and I just I knew I needed to change things up.

I am 38 years old. I don't have any children. My husband has a really great stable job and so it was like why not just try?

**Aimee:** Heck yeah. I know this is totally off script but I think that a lot of my listeners get to this point in their life and I think the next thing it's like what do I do next? So, I figure out that I'm not where I want to be. What's my next step? What was your next step?

**Elizabeth:** My next step was let's go try something for me. Right. So, I had some really amazing female leaders that were in my sphere for a very long time. Some I worked with, some I did not work with, some I contracted with or hired with, you know, so to speak. And I looked at some of them and I was like, well, I can do that. Why can't I do that? Why can't I go to other organizations and add value through training and through meaningful engagement and conversation?

I'm already doing it internally here. Why am I giving my best self to a business when I can the business can be me, right? And so that's really I spent a good year kind of methodically plotting my escape to figure out, okay, well, what is it that I'm going to do? What do I want to do? Are there people out there who would be willing to buy from me? If they do buy from me, what am I going to price things at? What does that plan look like?

And so that's how I ended up getting into the corporate bestie. And I really focus on I do sales training, I do leadership development training, and then I of course focus on leadership brand. And that this is the stuff that I'm so passionate about and I love delivering it to people who want it who want to receive it. You know what I mean?

**Aimee:** I love it. Yeah, I totally know what you mean. I mean it when you do what you love to do there, it just feels different than when you're just going through the motions for someone else. You know, when you can really put your focus on something you're super passionate about, it's a different kind of life. That's what the manager is all about. It's how can we help other women find their freedom, you know, and you've been able to find your freedom with the corporate bestie, and it's so exciting. I love it. It just sparks me. I was thinking about it on the way over and I'm like, "Oh my gosh." But you did it, too. You know, you broke free. You broke free and now you have your own time. How do you manage your schedule now that you're a free woman?

**Elizabeth:** I don't know that I've cracked that nut yet. So, I am a work in progress and I try to take client and mostly customer calls and networking calls Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday. I try to be really thoughtful about doing business as usual administrative tasks, content development on Mondays and Fridays. I prospect at least an hour a day in the morning and also in the afternoon. And I'm trying really hard to stay in that structure. But I'm human and the older I get, I feel like I have self-diagnosed ADHD. I'm not even kidding. The cell phone it's on do not disturb all day because I can't look at it. And so for me to stay focused I need to follow some time blocking.

But yeah, you said something that I want to also just make sure your listeners understand and that is you know, there is freedom in this space. There's freedom in the other space too. When you're working in a corporate environment, there's just it's different freedom or there's different benefits or there's different reasons for why you stay. And ultimately everything is hard, right?

Corporate is hard. Entrepreneurship is hard. Having children is hard. Not having children is hard. Having parents is hard. Not having parents is hard. And so, if you're in a position to choose your hard, choose one that's going to fulfill you, right? That's going to just feel good because it's all hard. It's all incredibly difficult.

**Aimee:** True. It's so true. I love it. And that's so much of what you teach, you know, it's all around behavior. So you say leadership is a behavior, not a title. And I think that's all about, you know, you're choosing what behaviors we want to be in. Which hard behaviors do we want to focus on in life? But yeah, so let's get into that a little bit. So leadership is a behavior, not a title. Can you share an example of how showing up prepared can change the outcome in a tough situation?

**Elizabeth:** Oh gosh. I mean, showing up prepared is everything and preparation is just one piece of the pie when it comes to leadership. I have seen and again, I've trained a lot of salespeople. I've listened to a lot of phone calls. I have experienced and witnessed what lack of preparation looks like. And it is sloppy. It is not polished. It is not thoughtful. It's not customer focused. It's not anything really positive or good. It lacks executive presence and there's nothing leaderly about that.

Nothing says I don't value your time at all like not showing up prepared and it's almost like coming somewhere late or you know just not being on time. And so I think it's really important that especially women we're thinking about that, right? We have to be anticipating the needs of everybody around us, which again is stressful and can lead to burnout. But if we're incredibly intentional about how we are anticipating the needs of others, you know, we are showing up as that prepared partner and then people will want to work with us. People will want to be on our teams. We will get the clients that are attracted to us, you know.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. I can't tell. You know, did you know that I have six children?

**Elizabeth:** Yes, I did. And actually we didn't talk about that last time and I

**Aimee:** Preparation is key. Yes. If you're not prepared, you're just like, if I'm not 2 days ahead of myself with everything that needs to be done, you know, I need to know what people are eating, what kind of homework is done, what tutoring, what kind of sports activity is going on. But that's how I show up for my family is, you know, strategic, you know, my mom says I run the place like a general, but if I don't, you know, it's just a hot mess.

**Elizabeth:** Falls apart.

**Aimee:** I just it falls apart. I was gone for a week. I got a week off. I went to Costa Rica to see my best friend. I just got back on Friday and that's why I got the tan. Yeah. But I came home and it's like my kids were sick. You know, one of them didn't go to school at all for the whole week. And so now I'm playing catchup, you know, to get everybody back on track. Let's get back into the preparation instead of, you know, I don't like playing offense. I don't want to play defense. I want to play offense with the house. And you got to do that. If you're a manager, it's the same thing. You kind of want to show up. You want to anticipate the needs of your, you know, the people underneath you, you know, are you happy? Are you sad? How are you feeling? Is this too much? Is this not enough? You know, I see it. Yeah.

**Elizabeth:** Yeah. And I feel like a first-time manager probably isn't thinking about that all of the time. I just posted a video today online about how if you cancel a one-on-one with your direct report, you have to reschedule it because your direct reports, they really rely on that time with you. And, you know, even coming prepared to a one-on-one makes a world of difference to your team.

Nothing is worse than, you know, being I remember being, you know, an individual contributor and having some bosses, you know, throughout my career where I'm like, "Okay, I'm so excited. I have all of these things that I'm going to talk to her about and I have my bulleted list. I send them via email ahead of time and then I dial on and then my boss is driving in the car on the way to Wisconsin and she can't she can't see my screen right now, but you know, sure Liz, I'll help you." And it's just a little deflating, right?

And so there are, you know, and I'm not saying that we can't do that. I think that's the beauty of work life balance and hybrid working and working from home is that we can take, you know, calls from our car. But that's where managing expectations, anticipating the needs of others is really an important attribute.

**Aimee:** I love that. Yes, it's so true. Yeah, you really do have to anticipate. You know, someone comes super prepared and then you can't really participate in their preparation. That's another that's a real, you know, it's a failure to meet their expectations.

**Elizabeth:** Yeah, absolutely. But we're human, right? We're human and it happens. And but that's where I think also again with first-time managers, it's okay to create this safe space of being human, right? I'm not perfect. And it's okay to make mistakes and even as a manager, I don't know all of the answers sometimes. And hopefully the people you're hiring know more of the answers than you do, right?

**Aimee:** I love that. Yeah, that's such an important thing. I mean I in my business I often you know I don't know all the answers because it's a very high-level IT business. I you know and the the best part about it is I don't need to know because I've got the people that do.

**Elizabeth:** There it is. There it is. Yeah. Yeah. And you make that very clear, right? You're like this is not an Aimee task or this is not a thing for my brain space because you need to commit that brain space to something else.

**Aimee:** Yes. No, I'm not an engineer. I'm not an IT engineer. But if I need one, I know where to go. But I do think sometimes, you know, you can fall in the management trap of wanting to know everything, of wanting to be the one. And that's just such the trap, too. It's I see that so much around me where, you know, it's you want to be the you want to be the big guy who knows all the things and it's no, you want a girl boss. No one can know everything.

**Elizabeth:** Yeah. No. Well, and the people who have a perception of knowing everything, first of all, they're full of it. They don't there's no possible way. But also a lot of it is their intentional efforts into their leadership brand and how they're showing up and how they are being perceived. You know, one of the things that I talk about a lot is executive presence. There's a really great HBR article that I can share with you if you want to share with your viewers after this but it talks about gravitas which is charisma and you know the ability to be polished and then there's communication which is really being able to read an audience and read a room and how do you talk to how do you talk to a group of people while also talking to one person and then there's the appearance component which is you know you don't have to wear a suit and tie to have executive presence but you have to look the best at whatever it is that you do.

So if you're a personal trainer at the gym, you better be wearing the best personal training outfit that you can find other than the sloppy sweats that you probably want to wear when you're working out, right? And so I say all of this because again going back to people who talk about or it's perceived as though they know everything. It's not that they know everything. It's that they've really been intentional about how they're showing up in front of other people so that you feel that way.

**Aimee:** Ah, the brand. I love it. Leadership branding style. Got it. Absolutely. So, I wanted to shift gears just a little because you have such the knowledge transfer to give us on your past life. You spent years in the credit reporting industry. What did you learn there that women especially need to understand about credit?

**Elizabeth:** Oh gosh. Well, first I just want to say and money and credit and anything financial in that arena still I'm raising my hand it is an intimidating and uncomfortable conversation to be in. It is not easy to talk about all of the time and there is often just misogyny around the discussion too and whether it's intentional or not it's just a fact of the matter right women women were and have always been viewed as very different in the financial space as consumers as contributors as investors and so I think that it's important to say it's okay to not know in the spirit of not knowing everything it's okay to not know everything when it comes to this stuff.

The second thing I would say is I only know what I know about financial literacy and how to be self-aware of my spending habits because I was in a financial services institution that was highly regulated where I had access to my credit report. I had access to how businesses were looking at consumers. I had access to products that were being sold that to those businesses that would then impact me as a consumer like the text messages I get when fraud is suspected on an account. Right?

So there's a lot of stuff happening there and so you know we are experiencing the largest generational wealth transfer right now and that's where it's like women need to be ready because we are the ones that are receiving all of that wealth and so we have to invest smart. We have to educate ourselves on what to do with it. We have to be risk-aware. There is so much around money and it's not just money. It's financial. There is so much that we need to learn about how to just be financial contributors in the economy, in our households, for ourselves. And so there's so much there. We're scratching the surface on what I've learned and have taken away.

**Aimee:** That's just amazing. Why do you think this gap exists this the lack of education when it comes to credit and finance with women? You think it's just what's left over from the patriarchy?

**Elizabeth:** I do. I do. It's not just that. I think that's a huge contributor, right? We live in a system. Everything in our world in our lives. I mean, even the, you know, Monday through Friday schedule that I have is a system, right? So we are experiencing the byproduct of a very male-dominated patriarchal system. I think I was telling you last time we connected women didn't even get to get credit cards until the 60s and we couldn't buy houses and we couldn't even get divorced you know.

So we've come a long way, but there's still a long way to go.
